posts.title: The Art of Writing Clean Functions
posts.excerpt: Small, focused functions are the building blocks of maintainable code. Here is how to keep them sharp.
posts.author: Maya Chen
posts.content:
## Why function design matters

Functions are the smallest reusable unit of logic in your codebase. When they are well-named and do exactly one thing, the rest of your code reads like prose. When they sprawl, every change becomes a guessing game.

### Keep them short

A function that fits on a single screen is easier to reason about. If you need to scroll, you are probably mixing concerns. Extract helpers until each piece has a single, clear responsibility.

### Name things honestly

A function called `getUser` should return a user, not also send an email and update a cache. Surprising side effects are the number one source of late-night debugging sessions.

> Good code is its own documentation.

### Limit the arguments

When a function takes five positional arguments, callers have to remember the order. Prefer an options object, or split the function in two.

```js
function createUser({ name, email, role }) {
  // clear and self-documenting
}
```

Clean functions compound. Each well-crafted one makes the next feature a little easier to ship.

posts.title: Understanding Async/Await Without the Headache
posts.excerpt: Promises power modern JavaScript, but async/await makes them readable. Let us demystify the flow.
posts.author: Diego Alvarez
posts.content:
## From callbacks to clarity

Asynchronous code used to mean deeply nested callbacks, the infamous *pyramid of doom*. Promises flattened that pyramid, and `async/await` turned it into something that reads top to bottom.

### The mental model

When you `await` a promise, the function pauses without blocking the thread. Other work keeps running, and execution resumes once the promise settles.

```js
async function loadProfile(id) {
  const user = await fetchUser(id)
  const posts = await fetchPosts(user.id)
  return { user, posts }
}
```

### Handle errors with try/catch

Because awaited code reads synchronously, you can wrap it in familiar `try/catch` blocks instead of chaining `.catch()` calls.

### Run things in parallel

Awaiting sequentially is wasteful when calls are independent. Reach for `Promise.all` to fire them at once.

```js
const [user, settings] = await Promise.all([
  fetchUser(id),
  fetchSettings(id),
])
```

Master these patterns and asynchronous code stops being scary and starts being boring, which is exactly what you want.

posts.title: Why Static Types Save You Time
posts.excerpt: TypeScript feels like extra work until it catches the bug that would have shipped to production.
posts.author: Priya Nair
posts.content:
## Types are documentation that cannot lie

Comments drift out of date. Types do not. When a function signature says it returns a `User`, the compiler enforces that promise forever.

### Catch bugs before runtime

Typos in property names, forgotten null checks, mismatched argument orders. A type checker flags these the instant you write them, long before a user ever clicks a button.

### Refactor with confidence

Rename a field and the compiler walks you through every call site that needs updating. Large refactors stop feeling like high-wire acts.

```ts
interface User {
  id: string
  name: string
  email: string
}
```

### Better tooling everywhere

Autocomplete, inline docs, and jump-to-definition all get smarter when your editor understands the shape of your data.

The upfront cost of adding types is small. The payoff, measured in bugs that never happen, is enormous.
